summary: Fowler examines 'type-instance homonyms'—common words like 'book' that refer to distinct but related concepts (a literary work vs. a physical copy). He argues that while software modeling requires disambiguating these concepts with separate classes and names, we shouldn't expect to eliminate the ambiguity in everyday conversation because humans effortlessly resolve it. The key is awareness: recognize the homonym during modeling, create precise terms for the Ubiquitous Language, but apply precision with a light touch since the homonym naturally becomes a synonym for each new term.
key_points: Common domain terms often hide two related concepts—a type and its instances—that require separate classes in software but share one word in natural language
Humans automatically disambiguate type-instance homonyms in conversation, and forcing precise terminology makes speech awkward and unnatural
In Domain-Driven Design, both concepts need distinct names in code and Ubiquitous Language, but the original homonym remains as a natural synonym
These homonyms typically appear in terms central to a domain, making early detection during modeling crucial
Even experts fall into this pattern—programmers say 'add a method to an object' when they mean class, and everyone glosses over it
money_quotes: I can easily destroy the latter in my fire, but the former isn't touched by my flames and will doubtless last longer than I will.
The trouble with the type-instance homonym is that it occurs even within very tight bounded contexts.
The really interesting thing about this ambiguity is how *little* trouble it causes.
At some point in your career you've been the pedant who's pointed that out in a meeting. You've also effortlessly glossed over that point in countless conversations since.
We absolutely have to be aware of this homonym. We need to represent the concepts separately and with different names in our software.
It effectively becomes a synonym for each of the new terms.
Fortunately awareness is 90% of the battle here.
key_insight: Type-instance homonyms require precise disambiguation in software models, but attempting to eliminate them from conversation is futile—awareness and light-touch precision serve better than rigid terminology enforcement.
